location.origin: Formed in Seattle circa 1959. Homebase: 7757 30th Ave SW, Seattle (West Seattle/White Center corridor). CSV designates Central District -- reflecting the musical lineage (Dave Lewis covers, Central District R&B tradition) rather than residential address. First engine band from the 1960s teen-dance era.
location.formative: Afdem family living room (early recordings); Seafair Records / Tom and Ellen Ogilvy (label relationship); KJR radio / Pat O'Day (teen-dance circuit promotion); Spanish Castle Ballroom (Highway 99 between Seattle and Tacoma); Parker's (17001 Seattle-Everett Highway / Aurora Ave N); Birdland and Central District R&B clubs (musical source material); Dave Lewis Combo (repertoire influence); University of Washington (Larry Coryell attended while playing in the band)
location.career: Seattle teen-dance circuit (1959-1966): Spanish Castle, Parker's, school dances, KJR-promoted events. Shared bills with Beach Boys, Roy Orbison, Jan & Dean, Bobby Freeman, the Astronauts, Terry Black. Recorded at Acme Recording (engineer Fred Rasmussen) and Parker's (live remote, engineers Ray Van Patten and C.A. Noland). Labels: Penguin Records (1959), Guaranteed Records NY (1959, national distribution), Seafair Records (1960), Seafair-Bolo Records (1962-1965, primary label), Jerden Records (1966). National distribution via Atlantic Records for 'Leavin' Here' and 'Busybody.' Evolved into Springfield Rifle (1966-1971, Jerden/ABC/Tower/Burdette), then Springfield Flute. Archival releases: Memory Bank of Early N.W. Sounds (1983, mixed by Kearney Barton), CD reissue (2009). Reunion: Experience Music Project Grand Opening (July 28, 2000).

labels: Calla (CSV designation, unconfirmed in primary archival sources)

members.name: Jimmy Hanna
members.note: Defining vocalist. Bobby Bland-like tone. Left to front own 11-piece blues big-band. Now studio owner and music instructor in San Antonio, TX.

members.name: Larry Coryell
members.note: From Yakima's Checkers. Attended UW. Later called 'godfather of jazz-rock fusion.' Departed for New York September 1965. Died February 19, 2017.

members.name: Marcus Doubleday
members.note: Later played in The Electric Flag (Mike Bloomfield). Appeared on Bloomfield's solo albums. Died May 1, 2004.

members.name: Ron Woods
members.note: Jimi Hendrix schoolmate. Later: Buddy Miles Express, Pacific Gas & Electric, sessions with Jimi Hendrix at Electric Lady Studios (~1969).

members.name: Terry Afdem
members.note: Co-founder. Taught keyboards and mentored other musicians.

members.name: Tom Larson
members.note: Original leader. Approached Seafair Records with early recordings.

members.name: Harry Wilson
members.note: Replaced Coryell. From The Casuals. Continued into Springfield Rifle.

members.name: Tracy Townsend
members.note: Original drummer. Replaced by Ron Woods.

associated_musicians.name: Dave Lewis
associated_musicians.note: King of Seattle R&B. Central District organist. House band at Birdland (21st & E Madison). Performed 'Louie Louie' before the Kingsmen. The Dynamics covering his composition is the genre bridge from CD R&B to teen-dance circuit.

associated_musicians.name: Pat O'Day
associated_musicians.note: Created the teen-dance circuit that was The Dynamics' primary performance venue. KJR commanded 37% audience share. Spanish Castle, Parker's, and dozens of other venues.

associated_musicians.name: Tom and Ellen Ogilvy
associated_musicians.note: Their son Jimmy Hanna became The Dynamics' vocalist. The Ogilvy family label was the band's primary recording home.

associated_musicians.name: Kearney Barton
associated_musicians.note: Seattle's legendary recording engineer. Defined the 'Northwest sound' at Audio Recording studios. Recorded The Sonics, The Kingsmen, and scores of NW garage/R&B bands.

associated_musicians.name: Jimi Hendrix
associated_musicians.role: Ron Woods' schoolmate; later recording partner
associated_musicians.note: Woods and Hendrix attended the same Seattle school. Woods later did sessions with Hendrix at Electric Lady Studios. Hendrix's early bands (Velvetones, Rocking Kings) gigged at Birdland in 1959-60, same years The Dynamics formed.

associated_musicians.name: Mike Bloomfield
associated_musicians.role: Electric Flag leader (Marcus Doubleday's later band)
associated_musicians.note: Bloomfield recruited Doubleday on Coryell's recommendation. The Electric Flag and its blues-rock horn arrangements carry Dynamics DNA.

associated_musicians.name: Buddy Miles
associated_musicians.role: Band leader (Buddy Miles Express, where Ron Woods played)
associated_musicians.note: Hendrix's Band of Gypsys drummer. Woods' post-Dynamics career connected him to the Hendrix orbit through Miles.

associated_musicians.name: Rich Dangel
associated_musicians.role: Coryell's housemate / The Fabulous Wailers guitarist
associated_musicians.note: Coryell and Dangel shared a Seattle houseboat. The Wailers were the other premier NW band of the era ('Tall Cool One').

theory_nodes.concept: Proto-fusion laboratory (a single band containing the pre-differentiated raw material of jazz-rock fusion, blues-rock, psychedelic funk, and R&B; significance lies in what members became after dispersal; Larry Coryell = 'godfather of fusion,' Marcus Doubleday = Electric Flag, Ron Woods = Hendrix sessions; the band is the unsorted potential before genre labels exist)
examples: The Dynamics (degree 27) -- teen-R&B band containing future fusion pioneer (Coryell), blues-rock horn player (Doubleday), psychedelic-funk drummer (Woods), and blues vocalist (Hanna); all in one room at Parker's in 1964

Larry Coryell (external) -- played R&B guitar in The Dynamics 1961-1964; moved to New York 1965; replaced Gabor Szabo in Chico Hamilton's quintet; formed Eleventh House; later trio with McLaughlin + Di Meola; 'godfather of jazz-rock fusion'
Marcus Doubleday (external) -- Dynamics trumpet; recruited by Coryell's recommendation to The Electric Flag (Mike Bloomfield); Bloomfield solo albums; brought NW R&B horn experience to blues-rock
Ron Woods (external) -- Dynamics drums; Hendrix schoolmate; later Buddy Miles Express, Pacific Gas & Electric, Hendrix sessions at Electric Lady Studios
theory_nodes.description: The proto-fusion laboratory is the engine's first documentation of a band whose historical significance lies primarily in what its members became after leaving. The Dynamics' live recordings at Parker's (1964) capture a moment when jazz-rock fusion, blues-rock, psychedelic funk, and R&B were all being practiced in a single room by musicians who had not yet differentiated into those genre categories. Larry Coryell was learning R&B phrasing that would become the rhythmic foundation of his later fusion guitar. Marcus Doubleday was developing horn arrangements that would translate directly into The Electric Flag's blues-rock. Ron Woods was building the rhythmic vocabulary he would bring to sessions with Jimi Hendrix. The band is a snapshot of pre-genre potential -- the liquid state before crystallization. No prior engine degree documents a band whose primary significance is its dispersal products rather than its own output.

ancestors: Central District R&B tradition (Dave Lewis, Birdland, Jackson Street jazz scene) -- the musical source material that fed into The Dynamics' repertoire
KJR / Pat O'Day teen-dance circuit (1959-1970s) -- the performance infrastructure that gave the band a stage
Seafair-Bolo Records (Tom and Ellen Ogilvy) -- the label that documented the band's output
University of Washington (Larry Coryell's enrollment) -- the institutional draw that brought Coryell to Seattle
genealogy.relationship: The proto-fusion laboratory exists because Seattle in the early 1960s was a city where a jazz-oriented guitarist (Coryell), an R&B horn player (Doubleday), a rhythmically gifted drummer (Woods), and a Bobby Bland-style vocalist (Hanna) could all end up in the same teen-dance band. The laboratory was accidental -- nobody designed The Dynamics to incubate future genre pioneers. The city's compact music scene, the UW's draw for out-of-town musicians, and the teen-dance circuit's voracious demand for bands created the conditions. The significance was invisible in real time and only became apparent after the dispersal.

theory_nodes.concept: Central District genre bridge (the structural mechanism by which Black R&B from the Central District's club ecosystem was transmitted into the white suburban teen-dance circuit; Dave Lewis covers as the transmission medium; the same songs in different venues for different audiences; The Dynamics as the primary conduit band)
examples: The Dynamics (degree 27) -- predominantly white teen-R&B band covering Dave Lewis ('J.A.J.,' 1962) and performing Central District R&B repertoire at Spanish Castle, Parker's, and school dances for suburban teen audience

Dave Lewis (external) -- Central District organist, house band at Birdland (21st & E Madison), 'king of Seattle R&B,' performed 'Louie Louie' before the Kingsmen; his compositions traveled via cover bands into the teen circuit
Quincy Jones (degree 0) -- Central District's Garfield High School; the jazz/pop tradition that preceded and paralleled the R&B tradition The Dynamics channeled
Ernestine Anderson (degree 3) -- Central District vocalist; same CD musical ecosystem, different temporal slice and genre (jazz vs. R&B)
theory_nodes.description: The Central District genre bridge is the engine's first structural documentation of how music crossed racial and geographic boundaries in 1960s Seattle. The Central District's Black R&B clubs (Birdland at 21st and East Madison, the Black and Tan, the Golden Crown) produced a musical tradition centered on Dave Lewis, Ray Charles (who debuted at the Black Elks Club in 1948), and other Black artists. This music was performed in liquor-licensed adult venues for predominantly Black audiences. The same repertoire -- the same Dave Lewis compositions, the same Eddie Holland covers, the same 'Kansas City'-type R&B -- was performed by bands like The Dynamics on the white suburban teen-dance circuit promoted by KJR's Pat O'Day. The Dynamics were the primary conduit band: their cover of 'J.A.J.' received national distribution, carrying a Central District composition into markets that had never heard of Dave Lewis or Birdland. The bridge was one-directional in terms of recognition (the teen-dance versions reached wider audiences) but bidirectional in terms of musical influence (teen-dance bands like The Dynamics absorbed Central District phrasing and brought it back into their original compositions).

ancestors: Jackson Street jazz scene (1940s-1950s) -- the predecessor ecosystem that produced Quincy Jones, Ray Charles' Seattle debut, and the Central District's musical infrastructure
Birdland (21st & E Madison, 1941-1960s) -- the R&B club where Dave Lewis was house band and Jimi Hendrix's early bands gigged
Dave Lewis Combo (1954-1998) -- the band whose compositions ('J.A.J.,' 'Little Green Thing,' 'David's Mood') were covered by teen-circuit bands
KJR / Pat O'Day (1959-1970s) -- the radio station and promoter who built the parallel teen-dance infrastructure
genealogy.relationship: The genre bridge exists because 1960s Seattle maintained racially separated but musically connected performance circuits. The Central District clubs were the source; the teen-dance circuit was the distribution channel; bands like The Dynamics were the conduits. The bridge was not designed as cultural appropriation or cultural exchange -- it was the organic result of young white musicians hearing R&B they loved and performing it for their peers. The ethical complexity (white bands profiting from Black compositions in venues where Black audiences were not the primary market) is characteristic of American popular music in the pre-civil-rights era. The Dynamics' cover of Dave Lewis is structurally identical to the Kingsmen's cover of 'Louie Louie' or the Rolling Stones' covers of Chuck Berry -- genre bridges whose cultural politics remain contested.

theory_nodes.concept: Talent dispersal as legacy architecture (a band whose members scatter into multiple genre ecosystems, carrying shared DNA into separate lineages; the inverse of the convergence pattern documented at degrees 18-26 where multiple traditions flow into one artist/band; The Dynamics as divergence point, not convergence point)
examples: The Dynamics (degree 27) -- band disperses into: jazz-rock fusion (Coryell), blues-rock (Doubleday/Electric Flag), psychedelic funk (Woods/Buddy Miles Express/Hendrix), blues (Hanna), pop-rock (Wilson/Springfield Rifle), local education (Afdem brothers)

Jeff Lorber Fusion (degree 16) -- Talent Incubator parallel: Lorber's band also developed musicians who went elsewhere (Kenny G, etc.), but through a teaching/mentoring model rather than peer-level dispersal
Maktub (degree 18) -- The Workshop: ensemble whose significance IS the ensemble, not the dispersal. Inverse of The Dynamics pattern.
Shaina Shepherd (degree 26) -- The Transmuter: convergence pattern (multiple genres flowing INTO one voice). Exact inverse of The Dynamics' divergence pattern.
theory_nodes.description: Talent dispersal as legacy architecture documents a structural pattern where a band's historical significance is measured not by its own recordings but by the career trajectories of its former members. The Dynamics' album at Parker's (1964) is a good teen-R&B record; it is not a historically transformative recording. But the fact that the guitar player on that record later defined jazz-rock fusion, the trumpet player joined one of the first blues-rock bands with horns, and the drummer went on to record with Jimi Hendrix -- that dispersal pattern is historically significant. The band is a divergence point: a single source that feeds multiple downstream genre rivers. This is the inverse of the convergence pattern that has dominated degrees 18-26, where multiple genre traditions flow into single artists (Maktub, Shepherd) or collectives (Black Stax). The engine has been documenting convergence; now it documents divergence. The two patterns are complementary: convergence produces the sound of a city finding its synthesis; divergence produces the sound of a city sending its talent outward into the world.

ancestors: Central District R&B tradition -- the source pool from which The Dynamics drew their shared musical vocabulary
University of Washington -- the institutional draw that concentrated out-of-town talent (Coryell from Yakima/Richland) in Seattle
Pat O'Day teen-dance circuit -- the performance infrastructure that gave the band enough steady work to develop its members' skills
genealogy.relationship: The talent dispersal pattern requires two conditions: (1) a concentrated environment where diverse talents develop together, and (2) career opportunities elsewhere that attract individual members. 1960s Seattle satisfied both: the teen-dance circuit provided steady work (condition 1), and New York's jazz scene (Coryell), San Francisco's rock scene (Doubleday, Woods), and Los Angeles' session world (Woods) provided exit ramps (condition 2). The pattern is common in music history (The Yardbirds dispersing into Led Zeppelin and other bands; The Skatalites dispersing into reggae) but has not been previously documented in the engine.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- brass-section smear (twenty-seventh deployment; the entire horn section bends as a unit rather than individual instruments bending independently; trumpet and saxophone sliding between notes in parallel, producing a 'smeared' blue note that is wider and more unstable than any single-instrument bend; the collective blue note of a band that does not yet know how to leave space)
examples: The Dynamics (degree 27) -- Jeff Afdem (sax) and Marcus Doubleday (trumpet) bending together in the horn section; the smear is the sound of two brass voices sliding in approximate parallel, not precise unison
Shaina Shepherd (degree 26) -- metamorphic blue note: single voice bending through multiple genres (comparison: one voice transforming vs. two voices smearing together)
Black Stax (degree 25) -- collective-activist blue note: three simultaneous voices (comparison: distributed across three people vs. concentrated in a two-instrument horn section)
Thomas Marriott (degree 15) -- assertive brass blue note: single trumpet (comparison: solo brass precision vs. section-level imprecision)
theory_nodes.description: Twenty-seventh deployment of the blue note thread. The brass-section smear is the first blue note generated by a section rather than an individual instrument. Prior brass blue notes have been single-instrument bends: Marriott's trumpet (degree 15), Caliman's saxophone (degree 9). The Dynamics' horn section -- Jeff Afdem on tenor saxophone and Marcus Doubleday on trumpet -- bends as a unit, producing a 'smear' that is wider and more harmonically ambiguous than any single-instrument bend. The smear occurs because the two instruments are bending in approximate parallel rather than precise unison: the trumpet slides slightly faster than the saxophone, or the saxophone reaches a different target pitch, creating a moment of dissonance within the bend itself. This is the blue note of a live teen-R&B band at full volume in a roadhouse -- not the controlled studio precision of later recordings but the raw, live-remote imprecision that Kearney Barton and the Seafair-Bolo engineers captured. Twenty-seven mechanisms across twenty-eight degrees.

ancestors: Blue note -- all twenty-six prior deployments (degrees 0-26)
Blue note -- metamorphic (degree 26, Shepherd); single voice transforming
Blue note -- collective-activist (degree 25, Black Stax); distributed across multiple people
Blue note -- assertive brass (degree 15, Marriott); single trumpet
Blue note -- sax voice (degree 9, Caliman); single saxophone
Teen-R&B horn-section tradition -- two or three horns playing in loose unison/parallel at dance-band volume
genealogy.relationship: Twenty-seven deployments across twenty-eight degrees. The brass-section smear is the first section-level blue note, produced by the interaction between two instruments rather than the technique of one. It exists because The Dynamics were a live dance band, not a studio act, and live dance bands bend together with the approximate precision of musicians who are playing at full volume in a roadhouse. The smear is the acoustic fingerprint of the teen-R&B era: hot, loud, slightly out of tune, and more exciting for the imprecision. Twenty-seven mechanisms: bass-line (0), guitar (1), vocal (3), trumpet-warm (4), piano crush (5), altered voicings (6), trombone slide (7), bass fundamental (8), sax voice (9), alto cool bend (10), flute bent (11), bebop chromatic (12), modal integration (13), post-genre floating (14), assertive brass (15), synthesizer pitch bend (16), vocal glide (17), soul-vocal (18), collective-soul (19), americana-soul (20), funk-brass (21), vocal-funk (22), gospel-vocal (23), dynastic-soul (24), collective-activist (25), metamorphic (26), brass-section smear (27).

seattle_connection: Seattle's premier 1960s teen-R&B band (1959-1968). FIRST 1960s-ERA ARTIST in the engine -- temporal regression from 2010s back to 1960s. CENTRAL DISTRICT GENRE BRIDGE -- covered Dave Lewis compositions (Central District R&B king, Birdland house band) for suburban teen-dance circuit. PROTO-FUSION LABORATORY -- Larry Coryell (godfather of jazz-rock fusion), Marcus Doubleday (Electric Flag), Ron Woods (Buddy Miles Express, Hendrix sessions) all developed in this band before dispersing into nationally significant careers. JIMMY HANNA's 'Leavin' Here' (Eddie Holland/HDH cover) briefly distributed nationally by Atlantic Records. SEAFAIR-BOLO RECORDS primary label. PAT O'DAY / KJR teen-dance circuit (Spanish Castle, Parker's). LIVE ALBUM at Parker's (1964, BLP-8001) = definitive NW teen-R&B document. EMP GRAND OPENING REUNION (July 28, 2000) with Coryell, Hanna, Woods. THE LIQUID GURGLE as 28th acoustic element -- unsorted potential before individual genius emerges. THE CONDUIT as 28th artist-city pattern -- band transmits musical energy between circuits without generating its own. SEVENTH CONSECUTIVE E=4 (2:13:6:7) -- incubator-kinetic subtype. GENRE Stage 10: Soul/R&B (compound returns, historical root). KEARNEY BARTON mixed archival release (1983). HENDRIX CONNECTION via Ron Woods (schoolmates). DAVE LEWIS J.A.J. COVER = direct CD R&B lineage. CALLA label designation unconfirmed in primary archives -- possible national distribution connection. CD OSCILLATION ACCELERATES -- gaps 4, 7, 5, 1, now 1 again (degrees 25-27 are back-to-back-to-back CD). FIRST TEMPORAL REGRESSION -- 2010s-present at degree 26 jumps to 1960-1970 at degree 27.
